## Overview
Claude Opus 4.7 is Anthropic's most capable, generally available model, performing at the frontier across coding, agentic, and knowledge work capabilities.

Opus 4.7 handles complex, long-running tasks with rigor and consistency, pays precise attention to instructions, and devises ways to verify its own outputs before reporting back. The model also has substantially better vision: it can see images in greater resolution.

Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about Claude Opus 4.7:

## Release Details

Claude Opus 4.7 was launched on Thursday (April 16, 2026) after Anthropic launched Claude Opus 4.6 in February.

## Key Features and Improvements

Claude Opus 4.7 is better at software engineering, following instructions, completing real-world work and is its most powerful generally available model. The new model outperforms Claude Opus 4.6 across many use cases, including industry benchmarks for agentic coding, multidisciplinary reasoning, scaled tool use and agentic computer use.

Opus 4.7 is a notable improvement on Opus 4.6 in advanced software engineering, with particular gains on the most difficult tasks. Users report being able to hand off their hardest coding work to Opus 4.7 with confidence. Opus 4.7 handles complex, long-running tasks with rigor and consistency, pays precise attention to instructions, and devises ways to verify its own outputs before reporting back.

## Positioning vs. Claude Mythos

The company said it is an improvement over past models but is "less broadly capable" than its most powerful offering, Claude Mythos Preview. Opus 4.7's cyber capabilities are not as advanced as those of Mythos Preview (indeed, during its training Anthropic experimented with efforts to differentially reduce these capabilities).

## Availability and Pricing

Claude Opus 4.7 is available across all of Anthropic's Claude products, its application programming interface and through cloud providers Microsoft, Google and Amazon. Pricing remains the same as Opus 4.6: $5 per million input tokens and $25 per million output tokens.

## Adaptive Thinking Overview

Adaptive thinking allows Claude to dynamically determine when and how much to use extended thinking. Instead of manually setting a thinking token budget, adaptive thinking lets Claude dynamically determine when and how much to use extended thinking based on the complexity of each request.

## Model Support

Adaptive thinking is the recommended way to use extended thinking with Claude Opus 4.7, Claude Opus 4.6, and Claude Sonnet 4.6, and is the default mode on Claude Mythos Preview. On Claude Opus 4.7, adaptive thinking is the only supported thinking mode; manual thinking with budget_tokens is no longer accepted.

## How It Works

Claude Opus 4.6 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 use adaptive thinking, where Claude dynamically decides when and how much to think. Claude calibrates its thinking based on two factors: the effort parameter and query complexity. Higher effort elicits more thinking, and more complex queries do the same. On easier queries that don't require thinking, the model responds directly.

## Implementation

You can set thinking.type to "adaptive" in your API request, and you can combine adaptive thinking with the effort parameter to guide how much thinking Claude does.

## Performance

In internal evaluations, adaptive thinking reliably drives better performance than extended thinking. Consider moving to adaptive thinking to get the most intelligent responses.

The search results show information about Claude's "effort" parameter from the Claude API documentation. Here's what I found:

The effort parameter allows you to control how eager Claude is about spending tokens when responding to requests, giving you the ability to trade off between response thoroughness and token efficiency, all with a single model.

By default, Claude uses high effort, spending as many tokens as needed for excellent results. You can raise the effort level to max for the absolute highest capability, or lower it to be more conservative with token usage, optimizing for speed and cost while accepting some reduction in capability.

Some key characteristics of the effort parameter:

- It doesn't require thinking to be enabled in order to use it, and it can affect all token spend including tool calls.
- Effort is a behavioral signal, not a strict token budget. At lower effort levels, Claude will still think on sufficiently difficult problems, but it will think less than it would at higher effort levels for the same problem.

Medium effort is recommended as the best balance of speed, cost, and performance for most applications, suitable for agentic coding, tool-heavy workflows, and code generation. Low effort is for high-volume or latency-sensitive workloads, suitable for chat and non-coding use cases where faster turnaround is prioritized.

## Release Information

Claude Opus 4.7 was launched by Anthropic on April 16, 2026. It's available at Claude.ai, the Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry. Pricing remains unchanged from 4.6: $5 per million input tokens, $25 per million output tokens.

## Key Benchmark Results

**Coding Performance:**
- On SWE-bench Pro, Opus 4.7 scores 64.3%, up from 53.4% on Opus 4.6 and well ahead of GPT-5.4 at 57.7% and Gemini 3.1 Pro at 54.2%.
- CursorBench shows 70%, up from 58% on Opus 4.6.

**Vision Improvements:**
- Opus 4.7 now accepts images up to 2,576 pixels on the long edge (~3.75 megapixels), more than 3x the resolution of prior Claude models.
- For autonomous penetration testing visual-acuity benchmarks, Opus 4.7 reached 98.5% versus 54.5% for Opus 4.6.

**Agentic Reasoning:**
- A 13-point jump on multi-tool calling performance without tools and a 6-point jump with tools, representing the largest single-benchmark improvement in the full table.

## Competitive Position

Opus 4.7 exceeds its rivals — OpenAI's GPT-5.4 and Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro — on key benchmarks including agentic coding, scaled tool-use, agentic computer use, and financial analysis. However, this is not a model that sweeps every leaderboard, and Anthropic explicitly states that Claude Mythos Preview remains more broadly capable.

## What's New in Claude Opus 4.7

**Vision Capabilities:**
Claude Opus 4.7 is the first Claude model with high-resolution image support, with maximum image resolution increased to 2576px / 3.75MP (up from 1568px / 1.15MP).

**Context & Pricing:**

**New Tokenizer:**
The new tokenizer may use roughly 1x to 1.35x as many tokens when processing text compared to previous models (up to ~35% more, varying by content).

**Behavioral Changes:**
The model features more literal instruction following, particularly at lower effort levels, and will not silently generalize an instruction from one item to another. It has a more direct, opinionated tone with less validation-forward phrasing and fewer emoji than Claude Opus 4.6. There are fewer tool calls by default, using reasoning more, though raising effort increases tool usage.

**Security:**
Real-time cybersecurity safeguards: requests that involve prohibited or high-risk topics may lead to refusals.

## Vision & Image Processing
Claude Opus 4.7 is the first Claude model with high-resolution image support, with maximum image resolution increased to 2576px / 3.75MP (up from the previous limit of 1568px / 1.15MP).

## Enhanced Software Engineering Capabilities
Claude Opus 4.7 is better at software engineering, following instructions, completing real-world work and is its most powerful generally available model. The model handles complex, long-running tasks with rigor and consistency, pays precise attention to instructions, and devises ways to verify its own outputs before reporting back.

## New Tokenizer
Claude Opus 4.7 uses a new tokenizer, contributing to its improved performance on a wide range of tasks. This new tokenizer may use roughly 1x to 1.35x as many tokens when processing text compared to previous models (up to ~35% more, varying by content).

## Effort Control & Task Budgets
Opus 4.7 introduces a new xhigh ("extra high") effort level between high and max, giving users finer control over the tradeoff between reasoning and latency on hard problems. In Claude Code, the default effort level has been raised to xhigh for all plans.

On the Claude Platform (API), task budgets are launching in public beta, giving developers a way to guide Claude's token spend so it can prioritize work across longer runs.

## Context Window & Pricing
Claude Opus 4.7 provides a 1M context window at standard API pricing with no long-context premium, and is available at the same $5 / $25 per MTok pricing as Claude Opus 4.6.

## Behavioral Changes
The model features more literal instruction following, particularly at lower effort levels, and will not silently generalize an instruction from one item to another.
